title: GIT and $\mu$-GIT
speaker: Dietmar Salamon
abstract: In this lecture I will explain the moment-weight inequality, and its role in the proof of the Hilbert-Mumford numerical criterion for $\mu$-stability. The setting is Hamiltonian group actions on closed Kaehler manifolds. The major ingredients are the moment map \(\mu\) and the finite dimensional analogues of the Mabuchi functional and the Futaki invariant.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian Kähler action and complexification
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a closed Kähler manifold with a Hamiltonian compact Lie group action, an equivariant moment map, and the holomorphic action of the complexified group G^C.

proof_stages.label: μ-stability and symplectic reduction
proof_stages.summary: Semistability, polystability, and stability are defined by whether the G^C-orbit or its closure meets μ^{-1}(0), together with a discrete-isotropy condition, linking the GIT quotient to the Marsden-Weinstein quotient.

proof_stages.label: Moment-map weights without rationality
proof_stages.summary: Weights are defined as limits of moment-map pairings along imaginary one-parameter flows, generalizing line-bundle weights while allowing μ-GIT without the usual integrality/rationality hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Moment-weight, Mumford, and Hilbert-Mumford package
proof_stages.summary: The main numerical results are stated: the moment-weight inequality, Mumford’s invariance/equality of supremums, the Hilbert-Mumford criteria for the three stability notions, and a corollary on critical points of |μ|^2 minimizing along complexified orbits.

proof_stages.label: Identity-case moment-weight bound
proof_stages.summary: Along the curve exp(itξ)x the moment-map pairing with ξ is monotone increasing, and Cauchy-Schwarz gives the moment-weight inequality in the special case with no extra G^C-translate.

proof_stages.label: Kempf-Ness-Mabuchi convex functional
proof_stages.summary: A closed one-form on G^C descends to G^C/G and defines the finite-dimensional Kempf-Ness-Mabuchi function, whose geodesic convexity recasts the monotonicity calculation and whose boundedness/critical behavior reflects stability.

proof_stages.label: Chen nonpositive-curvature proof of the full inequality
proof_stages.summary: A geodesic comparison argument in the negatively curved space G^C/G controls the tangent directions connecting exp(-itξ), G^{-1}, and the basepoint, and comparing slopes of the convex functional yields the full moment-weight inequality.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness and remaining stability implications
proof_stages.summary: The talk sketches how Chen-Sun-type gradient-flow arguments produce an optimal dominant destabilizing weight for sharpness and how the remaining polystability and stability parts of the Hilbert-Mumford criterion are completed.

examples.name: Classical projective representation case
examples.note: The action of a complexified reductive group on a Hermitian vector space and its projective space makes the moment map and weights explicit through eigenspace decompositions, illustrating the abstract weight definition and the classical Hilbert-Mumford criterion.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to a complex/projective representation, writes the standard moment-map formula, decomposes into eigenspaces of Iξ, and identifies the weight as the maximal occurring eigenvalue.

passes.reason: The projectivized linear representation with its standard moment map is the ordinary classical GIT model, used as a baseline rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Hamiltonian circle action on the two-sphere
examples.note: A height moment map for an S^1-action on S^2, shifted so that zero is absent, critical, or regular, illustrates how the Kempf-Ness-Mabuchi function changes across unstable, semistable, polystable, and stable regimes.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the S^2 circle action concretely by viewing the moment map as a shifted height function and analyzing the cases where 0 is critical, absent, or regular and how these affect the Kempf–Ness function.

passes.reason: The standard Hamiltonian circle action on S^2 is a highly regular rank-one/toric symmetric model used to illustrate stability regimes, not as a failure case.
